result:
:::writing
The vending machine hums like it’s thinking, like it’s weighing your life choices against the price of a stale granola bar. It has that soft fluorescent glow that makes everything look a little unreal—like you’re already remembering this moment instead of living it.

You press B7. Nothing happens.

There’s always a B7. The one thing you decide on after standing there too long, pretending the decision matters. The one thing that refuses you.

You press it again, harder this time, as if conviction could translate through plastic. The machine whirs, considers, then settles back into its low, electric indifference.

A man passes behind you, glances at the machine, then at you, and gives the smallest shrug—as if to say, yeah, that tracks. Machines don’t break dramatically. They fail in tiny, stubborn ways. They withhold.

You think about picking something else. A6, maybe. Something you didn’t really want five minutes ago but could convince yourself to accept now. People do that all the time. Re-route, adjust, call it preference.

But there’s something about B7. It’s still there, visible behind the glass, perfectly attainable in every way except the one that matters.

So you wait.

The hum continues. The light flickers once, like a blink. For a second—just a second—you imagine the machine deciding. Not malfunctioning, not following code, but choosing. Deciding who gets what, when, and why. Deciding that today is not your day for B7.

It’s a ridiculous thought. You know that.

Still, you don’t press another button.

Eventually, someone else walks up. They don’t hesitate. They press A3. The machine responds immediately—bright, obedient, certain. A bottle drops cleanly into the tray with a satisfying clunk.

The person retrieves it and leaves without a second thought.

You look back at B7.

It looks exactly the same as it did before. Untouched. Unmoved. Possible.

You press it one last time.

This time, the machine wakes up. A deeper whir, a mechanical shift. The spiral turns, slow and deliberate, like it’s been persuaded rather than commanded. The granola bar tips, hesitates at the edge—just long enough to make you doubt—and then falls.

The sound it makes landing is louder than it should be.

You pick it up. It’s lighter than expected.

For a moment, you consider the idea that nothing changed. That it was always going to happen on that press, at that exact second. That all your waiting meant nothing.

But it doesn’t feel that way.

It feels like something, somewhere, gave in.
:::writing

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
